docs.title: SPIMemory
docs.text:
SPIMemory is an Arduino library for SPI Flash and SPI FRAM chips.

This manual is organized for:

  • firmware users integrating the library into applications
  • maintainers extending compatibility and behavior

docs.title: Start Here
docs.text:
• Use Quick Start to wire, initialize, and perform first read/write.
  • Use Troubleshooting when chips are non-responsive.
  • Use SPIFlash API and SPIFram API for the reference surface.

docs.title: Project Links
docs.text:
• Repository: https://github.com/Marzogh/SPIMemory
  • Published docs: https://chipsncode.com/SPIMemory/

docs.title: Page template
docs.text:
Each page should include:

  1. Purpose
  2. When to use
  3. Minimal examples
  4. Pitfalls / failure modes

docs.title: Writing rules
docs.text:
• Prefer short, direct paragraphs.
  • Keep one canonical page per API topic.
  • Avoid duplicate function declarations across pages.
  • Use fenced code blocks with language tags.

docs.title: Link policy
docs.text:
• Use absolute URLs for external links.
  • Use relative links for internal docs links.
  • Prefer canonical docs URL https://chipsncode.com/SPIMemory/ when sharing.

docs.title: Validation
docs.text:

mkdocs build --strict

docs.title: Docs Release Checklist
docs.text:
1. mkdocs build --strict succeeds locally.
  2. Navigation includes all new/changed pages.
  3. No broken internal links.
  4. CI docs-check workflow passes.
  5. Published site reflects merged commit.

docs.text: This section supports both quick navigation and deep call-by-call documentation.

docs.title: Start by Task
docs.text: Task Recommended calls Initialize memory chip begin, setClock Check chip identity getJEDECID, getManID, getUniqueID Find free write location getAddress, sizeofStr Write/read one primitive value writeByte/readByte, writeULong/readULong, etc. Write/read strings writeStr, readStr Write/read structs writeAnything, readAnything Write/read buffers writeByteArray/readByteArray, writeCharArray/readCharArray Erase flash data eraseSector, eraseBlock32K, eraseBlock64K, eraseSection, eraseChip Power control powerDown, powerUp Suspend/resume flash operation suspendProg, resumeProg Diagnose failures error, error(VERBOSE), functionRunTime

docs.title: Search Tip
docs.text: Use the docs search with the exact function name (for example writeByteArray) to jump directly to that function's dedicated page.

docs.title: How to read errors
docs.text:
• error() returns the last error code as a byte.
  • error(VERBOSE) prints a message to serial and still returns the code.

docs.title: Error Code Table
docs.text:
These values come from src/diagnostics.h.

Code Name Meaning 0x00 SUCCESS Function completed successfully 0x01 CALLBEGIN begin() was not called 0x02 UNKNOWNCHIP Chip ID not recognized 0x03 UNKNOWNCAP Capacity not identified 0x04 CHIPBUSY Chip remained busy past timeout 0x05 OUTOFBOUNDS Address exceeded memory limits with overflow disabled 0x06 CANTENWRITE Write-enable latch could not be set 0x07 PREVWRITTEN Target area already contains data 0x08 LOWRAM Low RAM condition detected 0x09 SYSSUSPEND Suspend/resume state error 0x0A ERRORCHKFAIL Post-write verification failed 0x0B NORESPONSE No chip response over SPI 0x0C UNSUPPORTEDFUNC Function unsupported by chip 0x0D UNABLETO4BYTE Could not enable 4-byte addressing 0x0E UNABLETO3BYTE Could not return to 3-byte addressing 0x0F CHIPISPOWEREDDOWN Operation attempted while powered down 0x10 NOSFDP SFDP not available 0x11 NOSFDPERASEPARAM SFDP erase parameters unavailable 0x12 NOSFDPERASETIME SFDP erase timing unavailable 0x13 NOSFDPPROGRAMTIMEPARAM SFDP program timing unavailable 0x14 NOCHIPSELECTDECLARED Custom SPI constructor missing chip select 0xFE UNKNOWNERROR Unknown error state

docs.title: Fast Debug Sequence (Beginners)
docs.text:
1. Call begin() in setup() and print getJEDECID().
  2. If zero or invalid, check wiring and chip-select pin.
  3. Call error(VERBOSE) after each failed operation.
  4. For flash writes, erase region before writing.
  5. Keep errorCheck=true until your setup is stable.

docs.title: Best Practices
docs.text:
• Keep a small serial log of operation + returned error code.
  • For production firmware, map key error codes into telemetry counters.
  • Use FlashDiagnostics example sketch for initial bring-up.

docs.title: Status
docs.text: FRAM support exists in the library and should be treated as a narrower compatibility surface than SPIFlash.

docs.title: Functional groups
docs.text:
• Initialization and identification
  • Primitive and typed read/write
  • Region/chip clear operations
  • Power control

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• FRAM write semantics differ from flash (no erase-before-write requirement).
  • Validate behavior on your exact FRAM part number.

docs.title: Signatures
docs.text:
• bool SPIFlash::begin(uint32_t flashChipSize = 0)
  • bool SPIFram::begin(uint32_t flashChipSize = 0)

docs.title: What It Is For
docs.text: Initialize SPI memory communication, detect chip identity, load capacity/page behavior, and prepare the object for read/write operations.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: • flashChipSize: Optional explicit size override for unsupported or custom chips. Leave 0 for automatic detection.

docs.title: Behavior Details
docs.text: begin() must be called once in setup() before any data I/O. It configures communication, verifies chip support, and caches capacity/ID information used by every later call.

docs.title: Return Semantics
docs.text: Returns true on successful initialization and chip identification. Returns false if comms/ID/support checks fail.

docs.title: Failure Behavior
docs.text: Failure usually indicates wiring issues, wrong CS pin, unsupported chip, incorrect voltage levels, or SPI bus mismatch. Check error(VERBOSE) immediately.

docs.title: Common Mistakes
docs.text:
• Calling memory I/O before begin() has succeeded.
  • Passing a custom flashChipSize that does not match real hardware.
  • Ignoring a false return and continuing instead of checking error(VERBOSE).

docs.title: Choosing Between Similar APIs
docs.text:
• Use begin(0) for normal auto-detection.
  • Use non-zero flashChipSize only for unsupported/custom chips where auto-detect is inadequate.
  • If begin() is unstable, fix wiring/clock before trying advanced APIs.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
SPIFlash flash(10);

void setup() {
  Serial.begin(115200);
  if (!flash.begin()) {
    flash.error(VERBOSE);
    while (1) {}
  }
}

docs.title: Signatures
docs.text:
• SPIFlash(uint8_t cs = CS)
  • SPIFlash(uint8_t cs, SPIClass *spiinterface) (supported boards)
  • SPIFlash(int8_t *SPIPinsArray) (non-standard SPI pin mapping)

docs.title: What It Is For
docs.text: Construct a SPIFlash object and define which chip-select pin and SPI bus/pins are used to talk to the flash chip.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text:
• cs: Chip-select pin used for the memory chip.
  • spiinterface: Optional SPI bus object (&SPI, &SPI1, etc.) on boards with multiple buses.
  • SPIPinsArray: Non-standard SPI pin mapping array (sck, miso, mosi, ss) for supported targets.

docs.title: Behavior Details
docs.text: Constructor selection is a board-integration decision: use default constructor on standard wiring, alternate bus constructor when sharing buses, and custom pin constructor when your board supports remapped SPI pins. Construction does not probe the chip yet; begin() does that.

docs.title: Return Semantics
docs.text: Constructors do not return values. They create the object and store interface configuration for later calls.

docs.title: Failure Behavior
docs.text: Misconfigured pins or bus selection are usually detected at begin() time via failed ID reads or error codes.

docs.title: Common Mistakes
docs.text:
• Using wrong CS pin relative to your wiring diagram.
  • Selecting alternate SPI bus constructor on a board/core that does not support it.
  • Passing custom SPI pin arrays that do not match board capabilities.

docs.title: Choosing Between Similar APIs
docs.text:
• Use default constructor when your board uses default SPI pins and only CS is custom.
  • Use SPIFlash(cs, &SPI1) when board exposes multiple hardware SPI buses.
  • Use SPIFlash(SPIPinsArray) only on targets that support non-standard SPI pin routing.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
#include <SPIMemory.h>

SPIFlash flash(10);              // default SPI bus, CS on D10
// SPIFlash flash(5, &SPI1);     // alternate SPI bus on supported boards
// int8_t pins[4] = {18, 19, 23, 5};
// SPIFlash flash(pins);         // custom SPI pin mapping on supported targets

docs.title: Signatures
docs.text:
• SPIFram(uint8_t cs = CS)
  • SPIFram(uint8_t cs, SPIClass *spiinterface) (supported boards)

docs.title: What It Is For
docs.text: Construct a SPIFram object and choose which SPI bus and chip-select pin are used to access FRAM.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text:
• cs: Chip-select pin connected to FRAM chip CS.
  • spiinterface: Optional bus pointer for multi-SPI boards.

docs.title: Behavior Details
docs.text: Construction only stores interface wiring. Hardware probing, device identification, and readiness checks happen in begin().

docs.title: Return Semantics
docs.text: Constructors do not return values; they prepare the instance.

docs.title: Failure Behavior
docs.text: Incorrect bus/pin choices usually surface during begin() with zero IDs or non-zero error codes.

docs.title: Common Mistakes
docs.text:
• Using incorrect CS pin or alternate bus for your board wiring.
  • Assuming constructor validates wiring immediately (validation happens at begin()).
  • Reusing examples with different board pin numbering conventions without adjustment.

docs.title: Choosing Between Similar APIs
docs.text:
• Use default constructor for single-bus setups.
  • Use alternate bus constructor only when your board/core supports extra SPI instances.
  • Pick constructor based on wiring first, not performance assumptions.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
#include <SPIMemory.h>

SPIFram fram(10);
// SPIFram fram(5, &SPI1);

docs.title: Signatures
docs.text: • bool SPIFlash::eraseBlock32K(uint32_t _addr)

docs.title: What It Is For
docs.text: Erase the 32 KB block containing the provided address.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: • _addr: Any address inside target 32 KB block.

docs.title: Behavior Details
docs.text: Use for bulk updates where sector-by-sector erase would be slower.

docs.title: Return Semantics
docs.text: Returns true when erase completes.

docs.title: Failure Behavior
docs.text: May fail on chips lacking 32 KB erase opcode or when write/ready checks fail.

docs.title: Common Mistakes
docs.text:
• Using 32 KB erase for tiny updates, causing unnecessary data loss.
  • Assuming all chips support 32 KB erase opcode.
  • Not preserving neighboring data in same erase block.

docs.title: Choosing Between Similar APIs
docs.text:
• Use eraseBlock32K() for medium-size bulk rewrites.
  • Use sector erase for smaller updates to avoid collateral data loss.
  • Use 64K erase when throughput matters and data layout allows.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
if (!flash.eraseBlock32K(addr)) {
  flash.error(VERBOSE);
}

docs.title: Signatures
docs.text: • bool SPIFlash::eraseBlock64K(uint32_t _addr)

docs.title: What It Is For
docs.text: Erase the 64 KB block containing the given address.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: • _addr: Any address within intended 64 KB erase window.

docs.title: Behavior Details
docs.text: Best for large contiguous rewrites and reducing total erase command count.

docs.title: Return Semantics
docs.text: Returns true on success.

docs.title: Failure Behavior
docs.text: Unsupported opcode/chip profile mismatch or bus errors can fail this call.

docs.title: Common Mistakes
docs.text:
• Erasing a 64 KB block when only a small region needed reset.
  • Failing to back up adjacent data in same block.
  • Treating block erase boundaries as page boundaries.

docs.title: Choosing Between Similar APIs
docs.text:
• Use eraseBlock64K() for large contiguous regions.
  • Use 32K/4K erase to reduce unnecessary data destruction.
  • Coordinate block boundaries with your storage partitioning.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
if (!flash.eraseBlock64K(addr)) {
  flash.error(VERBOSE);
}

docs.title: Signatures
docs.text:
• bool SPIFlash::eraseChip(void)
  • bool SPIFram::eraseChip(void)

docs.title: What It Is For
docs.text: Clear entire memory device contents.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: • No parameters.

docs.title: Behavior Details
docs.text: Destructive operation used for factory reset, clean benchmark setup, or major schema reset.

docs.title: Return Semantics
docs.text: Returns true when full erase/clear succeeds.

docs.title: Failure Behavior
docs.text: Operation can be long on large flash chips. Power loss mid-operation may leave partially erased contents.

docs.title: Common Mistakes
docs.text:
• Running chip erase in normal flow instead of explicit maintenance/reset paths.
  • Not warning users that operation is destructive and long-running.
  • Power-cycling mid-erase and assuming data integrity afterwards.

docs.title: Choosing Between Similar APIs
docs.text:
• Use eraseChip() for full reset/factory-clear flows.
  • Use section/sector/block erase for normal incremental updates.
  • Avoid chip erase in frequently called runtime paths.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
if (confirmReset) {
  flash.eraseChip();
}

docs.title: Signatures
docs.text:
• bool SPIFlash::eraseSection(uint32_t _addr, uint32_t _sz)
  • bool SPIFram::eraseSection(uint32_t _addr, uint32_t _sz)

docs.title: What It Is For
docs.text: Clear a contiguous region starting at _addr spanning _sz bytes.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text:
• _addr: Start address for region clear.
  • _sz: Region size in bytes.

docs.title: Behavior Details
docs.text: On flash, this resolves to sector/block erase operations and is essential before rewriting programmed bits. On FRAM, this is library-level clear behavior.

docs.title: Return Semantics
docs.text: Returns true when clear operation completed successfully.

docs.title: Failure Behavior
docs.text: Invalid ranges, protected states, busy chip state, or unsupported erase granularity can cause failure.

docs.title: Common Mistakes
docs.text:
• Erasing too small a region before rewriting larger payloads.
  • Assuming section erase aligns exactly to your object boundaries.
  • Not accounting for erase latency in time-sensitive loops.

docs.title: Choosing Between Similar APIs
docs.text:
• Use eraseSection() when object size is known and may span multiple sectors.
  • Use eraseSector()/block erases for explicit granularity control.
  • Prefer smallest erase scope that still covers your write region.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
uint32_t len = sizeof(Config);
flash.eraseSection(addr, len);

docs.title: Signatures
docs.text: • bool SPIFlash::eraseSector(uint32_t _addr)

docs.title: What It Is For
docs.text: Erase the 4 KB sector containing the provided address.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: • _addr: Any address inside the target 4 KB sector.

docs.title: Behavior Details
docs.text: Use this when data lives in one sector and you want minimal erase scope before rewriting.

docs.title: Return Semantics
docs.text: Returns true on successful sector erase.

docs.title: Failure Behavior
docs.text: Fails if chip is busy, write-enable path fails, address invalid, or erase opcode unsupported.

docs.title: Common Mistakes
docs.text:
• Expecting arbitrary byte-range erase; this targets 4 KB sector granularity.
  • Erasing wrong sector by passing nearby but unintended address.
  • Not checking return code before writing new data.

docs.title: Choosing Between Similar APIs
docs.text:
• Use eraseSector() for localized updates in one 4 KB region.
  • Use eraseBlock32K/64K for larger contiguous rewrites.
  • Use eraseSection() when payload size is dynamic.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
uint32_t addr = 0x1200;
if (!flash.eraseSector(addr)) flash.error(VERBOSE);

docs.title: Signatures
docs.text:
• uint8_t SPIFlash::error(bool verbosity = false)
  • uint8_t SPIFram::error(bool verbosity = false)

docs.title: What It Is For
docs.text: Read the last library error code. Use verbose mode to print human-readable diagnostics over Serial.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: • verbosity: Set true (or VERBOSE) to print error details in addition to returning code.

docs.title: Behavior Details
docs.text: Call this right after a failed operation to capture meaningful context. Error state is tied to the most recent failing operation.

docs.title: Return Semantics
docs.text: Returns numeric error code (0 generally means no current error).

docs.title: Failure Behavior
docs.text: Calling much later can hide root cause because newer operations may overwrite error context.

docs.title: Common Mistakes
docs.text:
• Reading error() long after the failing call, losing useful context.
  • Ignoring non-zero error values during early bring-up.
  • Using error() without VERBOSE while debugging first hardware setup.

docs.title: Choosing Between Similar APIs
docs.text:
• Use error(VERBOSE) during bring-up and troubleshooting.
  • Use plain error() in production telemetry when serial logging is too expensive.
  • Read error immediately after a failed call, not later.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
if (!flash.writeByte(addr, 0x2A)) {
  uint8_t code = flash.error(VERBOSE);
  Serial.print("Error code: 0x");
  Serial.println(code, HEX);
}

docs.title: Signatures
docs.text:
• float SPIFlash::functionRunTime(void)
  • float SPIFram::functionRunTime(void)

docs.title: What It Is For
docs.text: Return runtime metric for the most recent library operation, primarily for diagnostics and benchmarking.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: • No parameters.

docs.title: Behavior Details
docs.text: Use in test sketches to compare timing impact of clock speed, fast-read mode, and erase strategy.

docs.title: Return Semantics
docs.text: Returns elapsed runtime value of previous operation.

docs.title: Failure Behavior
docs.text: Interpret values carefully: runtime depends on operation type, chip state, and diagnostic compile settings.

docs.title: Common Mistakes
docs.text:
• Comparing runtimes from different operation types directly.
  • Benchmarking with unstable wiring and reading too much into variance.
  • Forgetting that diagnostics/build settings can affect reported timing.

docs.title: Choosing Between Similar APIs
docs.text:
• Use functionRunTime() for relative profiling on the same board/chip setup.
  • Use external timing instrumentation for cross-platform comparisons.
  • Compare identical operations only (for example write-vs-write).

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
flash.writeByte(addr, 0x12);
Serial.print("Write runtime: ");
Serial.println(flash.functionRunTime());

docs.title: Signatures
docs.text:
• uint32_t SPIFlash::getAddress(uint16_t size)
  • uint32_t SPIFram::getAddress(uint16_t size)

docs.title: What It Is For
docs.text: Get next usable address region for writing a block of a given size without hand-maintaining offsets.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: • size: Number of bytes you need reserved for the next object/buffer.

docs.title: Behavior Details
docs.text: Helps avoid overlap bugs by automatically advancing internal allocation pointer.

docs.title: Return Semantics
docs.text: Returns start address for the requested block.

docs.title: Failure Behavior
docs.text: If allocation wraps or requested size exceeds available strategy assumptions, subsequent writes may fail address checks.

docs.title: Common Mistakes
docs.text:
• Assuming getAddress() persists across reboot without your own metadata scheme.
  • Mixing manual offsets and allocator-style addresses accidentally.
  • Not reserving exact sizes for variable-length payloads.

docs.title: Choosing Between Similar APIs
docs.text:
• Use getAddress(size) for sequential app-managed allocations.
  • Use fixed addresses when you need deterministic on-disk layout/versioning.
  • Pair with sizeofStr() for variable-length string records.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
uint32_t cfgAddr = flash.getAddress(sizeof(Config));
uint32_t nameAddr = flash.getAddress(flash.sizeofStr(name));

docs.title: Signatures
docs.text:
• uint32_t SPIFlash::getCapacity(void)
  • uint32_t SPIFram::getCapacity(void)

docs.title: What It Is For
docs.text: Return total chip capacity in bytes used for bounds checks, random-address generation, and buffer planning.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: • No parameters.

docs.title: Behavior Details
docs.text: Use this when calculating safe address ranges: valid span is 0 to capacity - 1.

docs.title: Return Semantics
docs.text: Returns capacity in bytes.

docs.title: Failure Behavior
docs.text: Using hardcoded capacities causes address overrun on different chip variants.

docs.title: Common Mistakes
docs.text:
• Hardcoding capacity from datasheet instead of querying runtime value.
  • Using random address upper bound equal to capacity without considering payload size.
  • Assuming same capacity across all user hardware variants.

docs.title: Choosing Between Similar APIs
docs.text:
• Use getCapacity() for dynamic bounds-safe code.
  • Use constants only when targeting one fixed chip SKU.
  • Combine with payload size checks to avoid end-of-chip overruns.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
uint32_t cap = flash.getCapacity();
uint32_t addr = random(0, cap);

docs.title: Signatures
docs.text:
• uint32_t SPIFlash::getJEDECID(void)
  • uint32_t SPIFram::getJEDECID(void)

docs.title: What It Is For
docs.text: Read JEDEC triplet (manufacturer + memory type + capacity code) used to identify the chip model family.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: • No parameters.

docs.title: Behavior Details
docs.text: Primary identity check for memory chips. Many diagnostics and support flows begin with this value.

docs.title: Return Semantics
docs.text: Returns JEDEC ID packed into a 32-bit value.

docs.title: Failure Behavior
docs.text: Zero or unstable values indicate communication faults, wrong voltage, wrong chip-select, or unsupported devices.

docs.title: Common Mistakes
docs.text:
• Proceeding when JEDEC ID is 0x000000 or unstable.
  • Assuming every JEDEC ID is in the library support list.
  • Not logging JEDEC ID in support/troubleshooting reports.

docs.title: Choosing Between Similar APIs
docs.text:
• Use getJEDECID() as primary identity check.
  • Use getManID() only as a coarse supplement.
  • Use getCapacity() after successful begin for operational bounds.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
uint32_t jedec = flash.getJEDECID();
Serial.print("JEDEC: 0x");
Serial.println(jedec, HEX);

docs.title: Signatures
docs.text:
• uint16_t SPIFlash::getManID(void)
  • uint16_t SPIFram::getManID(void)

docs.title: What It Is For
docs.text: Read manufacturer identifier to confirm vendor family and validate bus communication.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: • No parameters.

docs.title: Behavior Details
docs.text: Useful during bring-up to verify chip is physically responding and not all-zero/all-ones bus noise.

docs.title: Return Semantics
docs.text: Returns manufacturer ID value.

docs.title: Failure Behavior
docs.text: Unexpected values usually indicate wiring/SPI issues or unsupported chips.

docs.title: Common Mistakes
docs.text:
• Using manufacturer ID alone to infer exact chip size/model.
  • Trusting unstable IDs without verifying wiring and power integrity.
  • Skipping JEDEC read when manufacturer ID looks plausible.

docs.title: Choosing Between Similar APIs
docs.text:
• Use getManID() for quick vendor sanity checks.
  • Use getJEDECID() for model/capacity-level identification.
  • Prefer JEDEC for compatibility decisions.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
Serial.print("Man ID: 0x");
Serial.println(flash.getManID(), HEX);

docs.title: Signatures
docs.text: • uint32_t SPIFlash::getMaxPage(void)

docs.title: What It Is For
docs.text: Return maximum page index/count based on detected flash capacity and configured page size.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: • No parameters.

docs.title: Behavior Details
docs.text: Useful for page-based diagnostics and page iteration logic.

docs.title: Return Semantics
docs.text: Returns max page value used by library page calculations.

docs.title: Failure Behavior
docs.text: Using stale/hardcoded page limits can cause out-of-range accesses.

docs.title: Common Mistakes
docs.text:
• Confusing page count with byte capacity.
  • Using page math from another chip without recalculating.
  • Assuming page operations are equivalent to erase boundaries.

docs.title: Choosing Between Similar APIs
docs.text:
• Use getMaxPage() for page-oriented diagnostics/tooling.
  • Use raw byte addresses for most application storage logic.
  • Do not substitute page count for erase-block planning.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
Serial.print("Max pages: ");
Serial.println(flash.getMaxPage());

docs.title: Signatures
docs.text:
• uint64_t SPIFlash::getUniqueID(void)
  • uint64_t SPIFram::getUniqueID(void)

docs.title: What It Is For
docs.text: Read chip unique identifier when supported by the specific memory device.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: • No parameters.

docs.title: Behavior Details
docs.text: Useful for device fingerprinting, provisioning, and differentiating units in production logs.

docs.title: Return Semantics
docs.text: Returns unique ID value, often 0 when unsupported/unavailable.

docs.title: Failure Behavior
docs.text: Not all chips implement unique-ID commands; a zero value may reflect unsupported feature rather than total comms failure.

docs.title: Common Mistakes
docs.text:
• Assuming all chips implement unique ID and treating 0 as guaranteed comm failure.
  • Using UID as security/authentication secret without threat modeling.
  • Changing provisioning logic without handling unsupported chips.

docs.title: Choosing Between Similar APIs
docs.text:
• Use getUniqueID() for unit fingerprinting when supported.
  • Use JEDEC/manufacturer IDs for chip compatibility, not per-unit identity.
  • If UID is unsupported, fall back to app-level provisioning IDs.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
uint64_t uid = flash.getUniqueID();
Serial.println((uint32_t)(uid >> 32), HEX);
Serial.println((uint32_t)uid, HEX);

• bool SPIFlash::libver(uint8_t *b1, uint8_t *b2, uint8_t *b3)
  • bool SPIFram::libver(uint8_t *b1, uint8_t *b2, uint8_t *b3)

docs.title: What It Is For
docs.text: Retrieve library semantic version components at runtime for debug logs and support reports.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text:
• b1: Major version output pointer.
  • b2: Minor version output pointer.
  • b3: Patch version output pointer.

docs.title: Behavior Details
docs.text: Writes version numbers through pointers so firmware can print exact library version in diagnostics.

docs.title: Return Semantics
docs.text: Returns true if version values were written successfully.

docs.title: Failure Behavior
docs.text: Passing null/invalid pointers leads to undefined behavior in C++ pointer semantics. Always pass addresses of uint8_t variables.

docs.title: Common Mistakes
docs.text:
• Passing invalid/null pointers for version outputs.
  • Printing version once at boot and forgetting to include it in bug reports.
  • Assuming runtime version is identical across all build environments.

docs.title: Choosing Between Similar APIs
docs.text:
• Use libver() when collecting support logs.
  • Use compile-time docs/changelog for feature planning, not runtime version alone.
  • Keep both runtime version and JEDEC ID in bug reports.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
uint8_t maj, min, pat;
if (flash.libver(&maj, &min, &pat)) {
  Serial.printf("SPIMemory %u.%u.%u\n", maj, min, pat);
}

docs.title: Signatures
docs.text:
• bool SPIFlash::powerDown(void)
  • bool SPIFram::powerDown(void)

docs.title: What It Is For
docs.text: Put memory chip into low-power mode for energy-sensitive systems.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: • No parameters.

docs.title: Behavior Details
docs.text: Use in battery-driven designs between memory operations. Always pair with powerUp() before next read/write.

docs.title: Return Semantics
docs.text: Returns true if command accepted by chip.

docs.title: Failure Behavior
docs.text: Unsupported on some chips or states; subsequent read/write calls fail while powered down.

docs.title: Common Mistakes
docs.text:
• Powering down and then attempting reads/writes without wake-up.
  • Assuming every chip fully supports low-power command set.
  • Not integrating memory wake timing with MCU sleep/wake flow.

docs.title: Choosing Between Similar APIs
docs.text:
• Use powerDown() for low-power idle intervals.
  • Skip power cycling if your workload is high-frequency and latency-sensitive.
  • Always schedule powerUp() before next access.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
flash.powerDown();
// sleep MCU
flash.powerUp();

docs.title: Signatures
docs.text:
• bool SPIFlash::powerUp(void)
  • bool SPIFram::powerUp(void)

docs.title: What It Is For
docs.text: Wake memory chip from low-power state so normal operations can resume.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: • No parameters.

docs.title: Behavior Details
docs.text: Call before any read/write after powerDown or deep sleep transitions.

docs.title: Return Semantics
docs.text: Returns true when chip acknowledges wake-up sequence.

docs.title: Failure Behavior
docs.text: If wake fails, subsequent operations may fail; check wiring/power timing and error code.

docs.title: Common Mistakes
docs.text:
• Skipping powerUp() after deep sleep before first memory access.
  • Assuming immediate readiness without checking first operation result.
  • Ignoring wake failures and misattributing later read errors.

docs.title: Choosing Between Similar APIs
docs.text:
• Use powerUp() after explicit memory power-down or deep sleep.
  • Skip only when chip was never placed in low-power state.
  • If wake is flaky, reduce SPI speed and verify power sequencing.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
if (!flash.powerUp()) {
  flash.error(VERBOSE);
}

docs.title: Signatures
docs.text:
• template <class T> bool SPIFlash::readAnything(uint32_t _addr, T& data, bool fastRead = false)
  • template <class T> bool SPIFram::readAnything(uint32_t _addr, T& data, bool fastRead = false)

docs.title: What It Is For
docs.text: Read raw bytes into an object of type T, reversing writeAnything.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text:
• _addr: Address where object bytes begin.
  • data: Output object reference to fill.
  • fastRead: Optional read acceleration mode.

docs.title: Behavior Details
docs.text: Only safe when T layout matches exactly what was written (same fields/order/types/packing).

docs.title: Return Semantics
docs.text: Returns true when object bytes were read successfully.

docs.title: Failure Behavior
docs.text: Mismatched type layout gives corrupted values even if API reports success.

docs.title: Common Mistakes
docs.text:
• Reading into a type layout different from what was written.
  • Assuming success return means semantic correctness of decoded fields.
  • Using packed/unpacked structs inconsistently across toolchains.

docs.title: Choosing Between Similar APIs
docs.text:
• Use readAnything() to reconstruct objects written by writeAnything().
  • Use typed reads for single values to reduce layout-coupling risk.
  • Use array reads when decoding custom protocol payloads manually.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
Config cfgOut{};
if (flash.readAnything(addr, cfgOut)) {
  Serial.println(cfgOut.gain);
}

  • uint8_t SPIFram::readByte(uint32_t _addr, bool fastRead = false)

docs.title: What It Is For
docs.text: Read one uint8_t value from memory at a specific address.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text:
• _addr: Address to read from.
  • fastRead: Optional faster read path where supported; validate on your hardware before enabling broadly.

docs.title: Behavior Details
docs.text: Reads raw bytes and decodes into uint8_t. Pair this with matching write APIs to avoid type interpretation errors.

docs.title: Return Semantics
docs.text: Returns decoded uint8_t value from memory.

docs.title: Failure Behavior
docs.text: If address is invalid or comms fail, returned value may be stale/default/invalid. Check error() after suspicious reads.

docs.title: Common Mistakes
docs.text:
• Reading with wrong type/API compared to how data was written.
  • Using out-of-range or stale addresses.
  • Trusting suspicious values without checking error state.

docs.title: Choosing Between Similar APIs
docs.text:
• Use this typed read only for data written with matching type/layout.
  • Use readAnything() for struct reconstruction.
  • Use array/string reads for variable-length payloads.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
uint8_t out = flash.readByte(0);
Serial.println(out);

docs.title: Signatures
docs.text:
• bool SPIFlash::readByteArray(uint32_t _addr, uint8_t *data_buffer, size_t bufferSize, bool fastRead = false)
  • bool SPIFram::readByteArray(uint32_t _addr, uint8_t *data_buffer, size_t bufferSize, bool fastRead = false)

docs.title: What It Is For
docs.text: Read a uint8_t span from memory into a caller-provided buffer.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text:
• _addr: Start address to read.
  • data_buffer: Output buffer pointer.
  • bufferSize: Number of bytes requested.
  • fastRead: Optional performance mode.

docs.title: Behavior Details
docs.text: Always size destination buffer to at least bufferSize bytes.

docs.title: Return Semantics
docs.text: Returns true when requested bytes were read into buffer.

docs.title: Failure Behavior
docs.text: If used as C-strings, ensure null termination is handled explicitly by your code.

docs.title: Common Mistakes
docs.text:
• Reading more bytes than destination buffer can hold.
  • Using wrong start address and mis-parsing shifted data.
  • Assuming data is valid without checking application-level framing/CRC.

docs.title: Choosing Between Similar APIs
docs.text:
• Use readByteArray() when you need raw bytes.
  • Use typed read APIs for direct scalar conversion.
  • Use readCharArray() when treating output as text.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
uint8_t rx[8] = {0};
if (flash.readByteArray(addr, rx, sizeof(rx))) {
  // parse rx
}

docs.text: • bool SPIFlash::resumeProg(void)

docs.title: What It Is For
docs.text: Resume a previously suspended program/erase operation.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: • No parameters.

docs.title: Behavior Details
docs.text: Pair this with suspendProg to complete long operations after temporary interruption.

docs.title: Return Semantics
docs.text: Returns true on successful resume.

docs.title: Failure Behavior
docs.text: Fails if no suspend state exists, command unsupported, or chip state invalid.

docs.title: Common Mistakes
docs.text:
• Calling resume without a valid suspended operation.
  • Assuming resume success without verifying final data integrity.
  • Ignoring failed resume and continuing normal writes immediately.

docs.title: Choosing Between Similar APIs
docs.text:
• Use resumeProg() only after confirmed suspend state.
  • If suspend is unsupported, keep single-operation flow without resume logic.
  • Validate final data after resume on your exact chip.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
flash.suspendProg();
// do urgent task
flash.resumeProg();

docs.title: Signatures
docs.text:
• void SPIFlash::setClock(uint32_t clockSpeed) (transaction-capable cores)
  • void SPIFlash::setClock(uint8_t clockdiv) (legacy cores)
  • void SPIFram::setClock(uint32_t clockSpeed)

docs.title: What It Is For
docs.text: Set SPI clock rate used for memory operations. Useful for stabilizing marginal wiring or increasing throughput after validation.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text:
• clockSpeed: SPI clock in Hz (for boards using SPI transactions).
  • clockdiv: Legacy divider constant on non-transaction platforms.

docs.title: Behavior Details
docs.text: Use slower clocks for bring-up and long wires; use faster clocks after diagnostics are stable. Clock selection affects both reliability and performance.

docs.title: Return Semantics
docs.text: No return value. Clock setting is stored and used by subsequent memory calls.

docs.title: Failure Behavior
docs.text: Too-fast clocks can cause intermittent read/write mismatches, ID read failures, and random PASS/FAIL behavior in diagnostics.

docs.title: Common Mistakes
docs.text:
• Setting SPI speed too high during bring-up on long/jumper wiring.
  • Changing clock and assuming old stability still holds without re-testing diagnostics.
  • Using board-specific clock APIs incorrectly for your core version.

docs.title: Choosing Between Similar APIs
docs.text:
• Use lower clocks for bring-up and long jumpers.
  • Raise clock only after diagnostics pass repeatedly.
  • Prefer reliability over peak speed for data integrity paths.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
flash.begin();
flash.setClock(8000000); // 8 MHz as a conservative starting point

docs.title: Signatures
docs.text: • bool SPIFlash::sfdpPresent(void)

docs.title: What It Is For
docs.text: Check whether Serial Flash Discoverable Parameters (SFDP) are present and readable on the connected flash chip.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: • No parameters.

docs.title: Behavior Details
docs.text: SFDP presence can improve compatibility and timing/erase parameter discovery on supported parts.

docs.title: Return Semantics
docs.text: Returns true if SFDP was detected/read, else false.

docs.title: Failure Behavior
docs.text: false may be normal on older/non-SFDP chips. It is not automatically a fatal error if chip ID and operations still work.

docs.title: Common Mistakes
docs.text:
• Treating false as immediate hard failure on chips that do not expose SFDP.
  • Skipping JEDEC/basic comm checks because SFDP is present.
  • Assuming SFDP guarantees all opcodes are supported the same way.

docs.title: Choosing Between Similar APIs
docs.text:
• Use sfdpPresent() to detect optional advanced parameter support.
  • Use getJEDECID() for baseline identification regardless of SFDP.
  • Do not gate all functionality solely on SFDP presence.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
if (flash.sfdpPresent()) {
  Serial.println("SFDP available");
} else {
  Serial.println("SFDP not available");
}

docs.title: Signatures
docs.text:
• uint16_t SPIFlash::sizeofStr(String &inputStr)
  • uint16_t SPIFram::sizeofStr(String &inputStr)

docs.title: What It Is For
docs.text: Compute storage footprint required by library string routines so allocations and erase sizes match serialized length.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: • inputStr: String value to size for storage/readback routines.

docs.title: Behavior Details
docs.text: Use with getAddress() and erase calls before writing strings to prevent under-allocation.

docs.title: Return Semantics
docs.text: Returns required byte count for storing that specific String.

docs.title: Failure Behavior
docs.text: If you erase/write fewer bytes than required, reads can truncate or include stale residual bytes.

docs.title: Common Mistakes
docs.text:
• Using String.length() instead of sizeofStr() for storage planning.
  • Erasing fewer bytes than string storage actually requires.
  • Reusing old allocation size after string content grows.

docs.title: Choosing Between Similar APIs
docs.text:
• Use sizeofStr() before writeStr() for exact storage planning.
  • Use writeCharArray() when you need explicit fixed-length text layout.
  • Prefer fixed-length arrays over String in hard real-time memory maps.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
String name = "sensor-A";
uint16_t sz = flash.sizeofStr(name);
uint32_t addr = flash.getAddress(sz);
flash.eraseSection(addr, sz);
flash.writeStr(addr, name);

docs.title: Signatures
docs.text: • bool SPIFlash::suspendProg(void)

docs.title: What It Is For
docs.text: Suspend an in-progress erase/program operation on chips that support suspend/resume.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: • No parameters.

docs.title: Behavior Details
docs.text: Useful for responsiveness: pause long erase, service urgent reads, then resume.

docs.title: Return Semantics
docs.text: Returns true if suspend request succeeded.

docs.title: Failure Behavior
docs.text: Not all chips support suspend semantics for every operation/state.

docs.title: Common Mistakes
docs.text:
• Assuming suspend is supported on every chip and operation state.
  • Calling suspend/resume without checking return values.
  • Suspending and never resuming, leaving long operation incomplete.

docs.title: Choosing Between Similar APIs
docs.text:
• Use suspendProg() only during long erase/program operations when urgent access is needed.
  • Use normal blocking flow if your application can tolerate erase latency.
  • Always pair with resumeProg() when suspend succeeds.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
if (!flash.suspendProg()) {
  flash.error(VERBOSE);
}

docs.title: Signatures
docs.text:
• template <class T> bool SPIFlash::writeAnything(uint32_t _addr, const T& data, bool errorCheck = true)
  • template <class T> bool SPIFram::writeAnything(uint32_t _addr, const T& data, bool errorCheck = true)

docs.title: What It Is For
docs.text: Write any trivially copyable object (struct, packed config, fixed-size type) as raw bytes.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text:
• _addr: Start address.
  • data: Object instance to serialize byte-for-byte.
  • errorCheck: Enables optional verification after write.

docs.title: Behavior Details
docs.text: Storage format is raw in-memory layout. Compiler packing/alignment/type definitions must remain consistent between write and read firmware versions.

docs.title: Return Semantics
docs.text: Returns true on full write success.

docs.title: Failure Behavior
docs.text: Changing struct layout between firmware versions breaks compatibility unless you version and migrate your data format.

docs.title: Common Mistakes
docs.text:
• Changing struct layout between firmware versions without migration.
  • Using non-trivially-copyable types/pointers inside persisted structs.
  • Forgetting erase-before-write requirement on flash.

docs.title: Choosing Between Similar APIs
docs.text:
• Use writeAnything() for stable structs/config blobs.
  • Use typed APIs (writeULong, writeFloat, etc.) for simple scalar values.
  • Use byte/char array APIs when you need explicit binary format control.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
struct Config { uint16_t mode; float gain; } cfg{2, 1.25f};
flash.eraseSection(addr, sizeof(Config));
flash.writeAnything(addr, cfg);

docs.title: Signatures
docs.text:
• bool SPIFlash::writeByte(uint32_t _addr, uint8_t data, bool errorCheck = true)
  • bool SPIFram::writeByte(uint32_t _addr, uint8_t data, bool errorCheck = true)

docs.title: What It Is For
docs.text: Write one uint8_t value to memory at a specific address.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text:
• _addr: Target start address in memory.
  • data: Value to persist.
  • errorCheck: When true, read-back verification is performed after write for stronger integrity checking.

docs.title: Behavior Details
docs.text: For flash, target region must be erased beforehand (typically 0xFF) for reliable programming. FRAM usually allows direct overwrite without explicit erase, but capacity checks still apply.

docs.title: Return Semantics
docs.text: Returns true when write sequence completes (and optional verification passes). Returns false on address, power-state, bus, or verification errors.

docs.title: Failure Behavior
docs.text: Common failures: out-of-range address, flash not erased, chip busy, powered-down device, or SPI signal integrity problems.

docs.title: Common Mistakes
docs.text:
• Writing to flash without erasing target region first.
  • Ignoring false return and not checking error(VERBOSE).
  • Reusing addresses without a clear storage map.

docs.title: Choosing Between Similar APIs
docs.text:
• Use this typed write when your payload is exactly this type.
  • Use writeAnything() for composite structs.
  • Use array/string writes when storing variable-length data.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
uint32_t addr = 0;
// flash.eraseSector(addr); // required for flash if not erased
bool ok = flash.writeByte(addr, 0x2A);
if (!ok) flash.error(VERBOSE);

docs.title: Signatures
docs.text:
• bool SPIFlash::writeByteArray(uint32_t _addr, uint8_t *data_buffer, size_t bufferSize, bool errorCheck = true)
  • bool SPIFram::writeByteArray(uint32_t _addr, uint8_t *data_buffer, size_t bufferSize, bool errorCheck = true)

docs.title: What It Is For
docs.text: Write a uint8_t buffer for binary/text payloads with explicit length.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text:
• _addr: Start address.
  • data_buffer: Pointer to source bytes/chars.
  • bufferSize: Number of bytes to write.
  • errorCheck: Optional post-write verification.

docs.title: Behavior Details
docs.text: For flash, erase destination region first. Ensure pointer remains valid for full call duration.

docs.title: Return Semantics
docs.text: Returns true when all requested bytes were written successfully.

docs.title: Failure Behavior
docs.text: Failures come from invalid pointers, address overflow, erase prerequisites (flash), or bus errors.

docs.title: Common Mistakes
docs.text:
• Passing buffer length that does not match real payload size.
  • Forgetting erase-before-write on flash region.
  • Using invalid/temporary buffer pointers.

docs.title: Choosing Between Similar APIs
docs.text:
• Use writeByteArray() for binary payloads and packet buffers.
  • Use writeCharArray() for text buffers where character semantics matter.
  • Use writeAnything() for typed structs with stable layout.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
uint8_t payload[8] = {1,2,3,4,5,6,7,8};
flash.eraseSection(addr, sizeof(payload));
flash.writeByteArray(addr, payload, sizeof(payload));

docs.title: Signatures
docs.text:
• bool SPIFlash::writeStr(uint32_t _addr, String &data, bool errorCheck = true)
  • bool SPIFram::writeStr(uint32_t _addr, String &data, bool errorCheck = true)

docs.title: What It Is For
docs.text: Write an Arduino String payload to memory in library-defined format.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text:
• _addr: Start address for string storage.
  • data: Input String to store.
  • errorCheck: Optional verification pass after write.

docs.title: Behavior Details
docs.text: For robust writes, reserve/erase enough bytes using sizeofStr(data) before writing. This avoids overlap/truncation when string lengths vary.

docs.title: Return Semantics
docs.text: Returns true on successful write (and verification when enabled).

docs.title: Failure Behavior
docs.text: Failures occur from insufficient erased space (flash), invalid address range, comms errors, or write-protect state.

docs.title: Common Mistakes
docs.text:
• Writing string data without reserving/erasing sizeofStr(data) bytes first on flash.
  • Changing string length over time without updating allocation/erase size.
  • Treating String storage as C-string without considering library format.

docs.title: Choosing Between Similar APIs
docs.text:
• Use writeStr() for Arduino String workflows with variable length.
  • Use writeCharArray() for fixed-size or C-string buffers.
  • Use writeByteArray() for non-text binary payloads.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
String msg = "Hello flash";
uint16_t sz = flash.sizeofStr(msg);
uint32_t addr = flash.getAddress(sz);
flash.eraseSection(addr, sz);
flash.writeStr(addr, msg);

docs.title: Common Tasks (Beginner Friendly)
docs.text: If this is your first run on real hardware, start with First Hour Setup, then use Example Sketches Guide when validating behavior.

docs.title: Save one number and read it back
docs.text:
uint32_t addr = 0;
flash.eraseSector(addr);
flash.writeULong(addr, 42);
uint32_t out = flash.readULong(addr);

docs.title: Save and load a string
docs.text:
String in = "hello";
String out;
uint32_t addr = flash.getAddress(flash.sizeofStr(in));
flash.eraseSection(addr, flash.sizeofStr(in));
flash.writeStr(addr, in);
flash.readStr(addr, out);

docs.title: Save and load a struct
docs.text:
struct Config { uint16_t a; float b; } cfgIn{7, 3.14f}, cfgOut;
uint32_t addr = flash.getAddress(sizeof(Config));
flash.eraseSection(addr, sizeof(Config));
flash.writeAnything(addr, cfgIn);
flash.readAnything(addr, cfgOut);

docs.title: Write/read byte buffers
docs.text:
uint8_t tx[8] = {1,2,3,4,5,6,7,8};
uint8_t rx[8] = {0};
uint32_t addr = flash.getAddress(sizeof(tx));
flash.eraseSection(addr, sizeof(tx));
flash.writeByteArray(addr, tx, sizeof(tx));
flash.readByteArray(addr, rx, sizeof(rx));

docs.title: Chip not responding
docs.text:
1. Verify wiring and CS pin.
  2. Ensure begin() is called in setup().
  3. Print getJEDECID() and error(VERBOSE).
  4. Run FlashDiagnostics example sketch.
  5. Compare results against Example Sketches Guide.

docs.title: Improve speed safely
docs.text:
• Read path: use fastRead=true selectively.
  • Write path: only disable error checks after full validation.

docs.title: SFDP note
docs.text: The library can support more flash chips through SFDP discovery when USES_SFDP is enabled in SPIMemory.h.

docs.title: FRAM Compatibility (documented as tested)
docs.text: Manufacturer Part Cypress/Spansion FM25W256

docs.title: Constructor Compatibility Notes
docs.text:
• SPIFlash(uint8_t cs) and SPIFram(uint8_t cs) are the default constructors.
  • SPIFlash(uint8_t cs, SPIClass *spiinterface) and SPIFram(uint8_t cs, SPIClass *spiinterface) are used on multi-SPI platforms.
  • SPIFlash(int8_t *SPIPinsArray) is intended for custom SPI pin routing (ESP32-style use cases).

docs.title: Before You Buy a New Chip
docs.text:
1. Check if the manufacturer is already in the tested list.
  2. Check if the part is SFDP-compatible (for flash).
  3. Plan to validate with FlashDiagnostics example and error codes on your target board.

docs.title: Addressing
docs.text: All APIs use a 32-bit linear address model.

docs.title: Erase Before Write (Flash)
docs.text: Flash writes require erased memory (typically 0xFF). Use erase APIs before writing to previously used regions.

docs.title: Overflow
docs.text: By default, operations can roll over from end-of-chip back to 0x00. Define DISABLEOVERFLOW to make out-of-bounds fail instead.

docs.title: Performance and Safety
docs.text:
• fastRead=true can speed reads on supported chips.
  • Write functions validate data by default; disabling checks increases speed and risk.

docs.title: Flash vs FRAM
docs.text:
• Flash: erase required, generally larger capacity.
  • FRAM: no erase needed for byte writes, lower write latency.

docs.title: Build Configuration
docs.text: This library is configured mainly through compile-time flags in src/SPIMemory.h.

docs.title: Compile-Time Flags
docs.text: Flag Default What it does When to use USES_SFDP Off Enables SFDP discovery logic for compatible flash chips Using unsupported but SFDP-capable flash parts RUNDIAGNOSTIC Off Enables verbose diagnostics messaging and runtime tracing support Hardware bring-up and debugging HIGHSPEED Off Skips pre-write blank checks (_notPrevWritten) Performance tuning after you trust erase discipline DISABLEOVERFLOW Off Disables address wraparound at end-of-chip Safety-critical logging where wrap is not acceptable ENABLEZERODMA Off Enables experimental SAMD DMA path Advanced tuning on SAMD if validated on your board

docs.title: Runtime Configuration Calls
docs.text:
• begin(chipSize) for optional explicit capacity override.
  • setClock(clockSpeed) for SPI speed tuning.
  • Alternate constructor variants for non-default SPI bus or custom pins.

docs.title: Default SPI
docs.text:
• SPIFlash flash(csPin);
  • SPIFram fram(csPin);

docs.title: Alternate SPI interface (supported platforms)
docs.text:
• SPIFlash flash(csPin, &SPI1);
  • SPIFram fram(csPin, &SPI1);

docs.title: Custom SPI pins (ESP32-oriented)
docs.text: • SPIFlash flash(spiPinsArray); where array order is sck, miso, mosi, ss.

docs.title: Recommended beginner defaults
docs.text:
• Keep all compile-time flags at default.
  • Keep write errorCheck=true.
  • Enable RUNDIAGNOSTIC only while debugging.

docs.title: Example Sketches Guide (Troubleshooting + Learning)
docs.text: The bundled examples are one of the best parts of this library. This page explains what each one does, what success looks like, and what failures mean.

docs.title: Exact test inventory (every operation it executes)
docs.text:
Identity and metadata (from getID()):

  • flash.libver(&_ver, &_subver, &_bugfix) (when LIBVER is enabled)
  • flash.getJEDECID()
  • flash.getCapacity()
  • flash.getMaxPage()
  • flash.getUniqueID()

Power tests:

  • flash.powerDown()
  • flash.powerUp()

Erase tests:

  • flash.eraseChip()
  • flash.eraseSection(_addr, KB(72))
  • flash.eraseBlock64K(_addr)
  • flash.eraseBlock32K(_addr)
  • flash.eraseSector(_addr) (4 KB)

Primitive data write/read tests:

  • flash.writeByte(addr, 35) + flash.readByte(addr)
  • flash.writeChar(addr, -110) + flash.readChar(addr)
  • flash.writeWord(addr, 4520) + flash.readWord(addr)
  • flash.writeShort(addr, -1250) + flash.readShort(addr)
  • flash.writeULong(addr, 876532) + flash.readULong(addr)
  • flash.writeLong(addr, -10959) + flash.readLong(addr)
  • flash.writeFloat(addr, 3.14) + flash.readFloat(addr)

Higher-level data tests:

  • flash.writeStr(addr, "This is a test String 123!@#") + flash.readStr(addr, _data)
  • flash.writeAnything(addr, testStruct) + flash.readAnything(addr, testStructOut)
  • flash.writeByteArray(addr, _d, 256) + flash.readByteArray(addr, _data, 256)

Timing and diagnostics:

  • flash.functionRunTime() is sampled after each operation to print runtime columns.
  • PASS/FAIL is decided by value comparison in each test function.

docs.title: Why run it first
docs.text: It gives broad signal quickly: if this sketch fails, fix hardware/config before writing app logic.

docs.title: Expected success signs
docs.text:
• JEDEC ID prints non-zero.
  • Function tests report pass.
  • Read values match written values.

docs.title: Expected serial output (sample)
docs.text:
Initialising..........

SPIMemory Library version: 3.x.x

JEDEC ID: 0xEF4017
Man ID: 0xEF
Memory ID: 0x40
Capacity: 8388608
Max Pages: 32768
Unique ID: ...

----------------------------------------------------------------
                        Testing library code
----------------------------------------------------------------
...
Byte        PASS
Char        PASS
Word        PASS
Short       PASS
ULong       PASS
Long        PASS
Float       PASS
Struct      PASS
Array       PASS
String      PASS

docs.title: Warning output to watch for
docs.text:
No comms. Check wiring. Is chip supported?

or repeated FAIL rows in erase/data sections.

docs.title: Common failure patterns
docs.text:
• Fails immediately: wiring/CS/board SPI mismatch.
  • Write tests fail: region not erased, WP/HOLD pin state, unstable power.
  • Mixed intermittent failures: clock too fast, shared SPI bus interference.

docs.title: What it is
docs.text: Interactive serial command tester for direct operation-by-operation checks.

docs.title: Best use
docs.text: Use this when you want to isolate a single failing operation (writeByte, eraseSector, readStr, etc.) without full diagnostics noise.

docs.title: Full command map (all interactive commands)
docs.text:
1. getJEDECID and component ID bytes
  2. writeByte(addr, byte)
  3. readByte(addr)
  4. writeWord(addr, word)
  5. readWord(addr)
  6. writeStr(addr, inputString)
  7. readStr(addr, outputString)
  8. writeByteArray(addr, pageBuffer, SPI_PAGESIZE) where pageBuffer is 0..255
  9. readByteArray(addr, data_buffer, SPI_PAGESIZE) via printPage
  10. Full-chip dump: loops through flash and calls readByteArray repeatedly in printAllPages
  11. eraseSector(addr) (4 KB)
  12. eraseBlock32K(addr)
  13. eraseBlock64K(addr)
  14. eraseChip()

docs.title: Tips
docs.text:
• Set Serial Monitor to expected line ending mode from sketch notes.
  • Test simple operations first (getID, writeByte + readByte).
  • Move to erase and string operations after basics pass.

docs.title: Expected serial output (sample)
docs.text:
On boot:

Initialising..........
...
Please pick from the following commands...
1. getID
2. writeByte [address] [byte]
3. readByte [address]
...
14. Erase Chip

When running command 1:

Function 1 : Get JEDEC ID
Manufacturer ID: efh
Memory Type: 40h
Capacity: 17h
JEDEC ID: ef4017h

When running 2 then 3 on same address:

... has been written to address ...

Function 3 : Read Byte
The byte at address ... is: ...

docs.title: What it teaches
docs.text: How to use getAddress() and sizeofStr() to allocate addresses safely without hardcoding offsets.

docs.title: Exact API calls used
docs.text:
• Allocation:
  • flash.getAddress(sizeof(byte))
  • flash.getAddress(sizeof(float))
  • flash.getAddress(flash.sizeofStr(testStr[i]))
  • Byte I/O:
  • flash.writeByte(byteAddr[i], testByte[i])
  • flash.readByte(byteAddr[i])
  • Float I/O:
  • flash.writeFloat(floatAddr[i], testFloat[i])
  • flash.readFloat(floatAddr[i])
  • String I/O:
  • flash.writeStr(strAddr[i], testStr[i])
  • flash.readStr(strAddr[i], _string)

docs.title: Troubleshooting value
docs.text: If this fails, you may have pre-written data conflicts or boundary behavior confusion.

docs.title: Expected serial output (sample)
docs.text:
Initialising Flash memory..........

Byte Address 0 : 0x...
Byte Address 1 : 0x...
Float Address 0 : 0x...
String Address 0 : 0x...

3 written to 0x...
3 read from 0x...
3.1415 written to 0x...
3.1415 read from 0x...
'Test String 0' written to 0x...
'Test String 0' read from 0x...

Addresses should be valid hex values and read-back values should match writes.

docs.title: What it teaches
docs.text: Simple writeStr/readStr workflow.

docs.title: Exact API calls used
docs.text:
• flash.getCapacity() (for random address generation)
  • flash.writeStr(strAddr, inputString)
  • flash.readStr(strAddr, outputString)
  • flash.eraseSector(strAddr) (cleanup after test)

docs.title: Troubleshooting value
docs.text: Great for catching string-specific issues (length metadata, readback integrity, sector erase assumptions).

docs.title: Expected serial output (sample)
docs.text:
Written string: This is a test String
To address: 123456
Read string: This is a test String
From address: 123456

The written/read strings should be identical and the address should match in both lines.

docs.title: What it teaches
docs.text: Write/read complete structs repeatedly, including nested members and arrays.

docs.title: Exact API calls used
docs.text:
• flash.getCapacity() (random target address range)
  • flash.eraseSection(_addr, sizeof(configurationIn))
  • flash.writeAnything(_addr, configurationIn)
  • flash.readAnything(_addr, configurationOut)

Loop behavior detail:

  • Runs NUMBEROFREPEATS iterations (100 by default).
  • Tracks eraseCount, writeCount, errorCount, readCount.
  • Final summary prints all counters.

docs.title: Troubleshooting value
docs.text: Use this for data-model persistence validation. If this fails while primitive tests pass, inspect struct layout assumptions and erase coverage.

docs.title: Expected serial output (sample)
docs.text:
Initialising Flash memory..........
1
2
3
...
100

----------------------------------------------------------------
                Final results
----------------------------------------------------------------
No. of successful erases:          100
No. of successful writes:          100
No. of errors generated:           0 (errorCheck function failures)
No. of successful reads:           100

Counts should be close to NUMBEROFREPEATS (default 100), with errors at 0.

docs.title: What it is
docs.text: Helper routines used by diagnostics output (formatting, timing presentation, pass/fail display).

docs.title: Why it matters
docs.text: Helps you understand what diagnostics output lines map to which underlying test sections.

docs.title: Expected serial behavior
docs.text:
This file does not run as a standalone sketch. It provides helper print functions used by FlashDiagnostics.ino, including:

  • line separators (printLine)
  • PASS/FAIL formatting (pass)
  • runtime formatting (printTimer, printTime)
  • ID printing (getID, printUniqueID)

If formatting looks wrong in diagnostics output, this helper file is the first place to inspect.

docs.title: Helper functions defined in this file
docs.text:
• Output helpers: printLine, printTab, printTime, printTimer, pass
  • Identity helpers: printUniqueID, getID
  • Test routines:
  • powerDownTest, powerUpTest
  • eraseChipTest, eraseSectionTest, eraseBlock64KTest, eraseBlock32KTest, eraseSectorTest
  • byteTest, charTest, wordTest, shortTest, uLongTest, longTest, floatTest, structTest, arrayTest, stringTest

docs.title: Recommended order for beginners
docs.text:
1. FlashDiagnostics.ino
  2. TestFlash.ino (targeted operation isolation)
  3. readWriteString.ino
  4. getAddressEx.ino
  5. Struct_writer.ino

docs.title: What to capture when asking for help
docs.text:
• Board and core version
  • Chip part number
  • Wiring summary (including WP/HOLD if flash)
  • Example sketch name
  • Error code (error() value) and serial output snippet

docs.title: First Hour Setup (Arduino Beginner Path)
docs.text: This walkthrough is for first-time users bringing up an external SPI memory chip.

docs.title: 0-10 min: Hardware sanity check
docs.text:
At minimum, connect:

  • VCC and GND
  • SCK, MISO, MOSI
  • CS (chip select)

docs.title: Flash-specific pin note
docs.text: Many flash chips also expose WP (write protect) and HOLD. If these are floating, writes can fail or communication can be unstable. Check your chip datasheet and set valid levels.

docs.title: ESP32 note
docs.text: Some ESP32 boards already use onboard flash on default SPI pins. Use an explicit CS pin and, if needed, the custom pin constructor.

docs.title: 10-20 min: Minimal identity test
docs.text:
Upload a minimal sketch that does:

  1. begin()
  2. getJEDECID()
  3. error(VERBOSE) on failure
#include <SPIMemory.h>

SPIFlash flash(10);

void setup() {
  Serial.begin(115200);
  while (!Serial) {}

  if (!flash.begin()) {
    Serial.print("begin failed, err=0x");
    Serial.println(flash.error(VERBOSE), HEX);
    return;
  }

  Serial.print("JEDEC: 0x");
  Serial.println(flash.getJEDECID(), HEX);
}

void loop() {}

If JEDEC is zero or invalid, stop and re-check wiring and CS pin.

docs.title: 20-35 min: First write/read
docs.text:
For flash:

  1. Erase region (eraseSector)
  2. Write one value (writeULong)
  3. Read back (readULong)
uint32_t addr = 0;
flash.eraseSector(addr);
flash.writeULong(addr, 123456789UL);
uint32_t out = flash.readULong(addr);

docs.title: 35-50 min: Run diagnostics sketch
docs.text:
Run examples/FlashDiagnostics/FlashDiagnostics.ino.

  • This validates many read/write/erase and power operations.
  • It is the fastest way to separate hardware issues from application-code issues.

docs.title: 50-60 min: Move to your real use case
docs.text:
Pick one:

  • Logging numbers: use primitive write/read calls.
  • Saving settings: use writeAnything/readAnything with a struct.
  • Storing user text: use writeStr/readStr.

Then use Common Tasks and Example Sketches Guide.

docs.title: Who this is for
docs.text:
If you are new to Arduino external memory chips, start here.

For a step-by-step first session, use First Hour Setup.

docs.title: Arduino Library Manager
docs.text:
1. Open Arduino IDE.
  2. Go to Sketch > Include Library > Manage Libraries.
  3. Search for SPIMemory.
  4. Install latest version.

docs.title: ZIP install
docs.text:
1. Download repository ZIP.
  2. Rename extracted folder to SPIMemory.
  3. Move it to your Arduino libraries folder.

docs.title: 2. Wire the chip
docs.text:
Connect at minimum:

  • SCK
  • MISO
  • MOSI
  • CS (chip select)
  • VCC
  • GND

For flash chips, ensure HOLD and WP pins are pulled to valid logic levels as required by datasheet.

docs.title: 3. First flash sketch
docs.text:
#include <SPIMemory.h>

SPIFlash flash(10); // CS pin

void setup() {
  Serial.begin(115200);

  if (!flash.begin()) {
    Serial.print("begin failed, err=0x");
    Serial.println(flash.error(), HEX);
    return;
  }

  uint32_t addr = 0;
  flash.eraseSector(addr);

  flash.writeULong(addr, 123456789UL);
  uint32_t out = flash.readULong(addr);

  Serial.print("Read: ");
  Serial.println(out);
}

void loop() {}

docs.title: 4. Validate using bundled examples
docs.text:
Recommended beginner order:

  1. examples/FlashDiagnostics/FlashDiagnostics.ino
  2. examples/readWriteString/readWriteString.ino
  3. examples/getAddressEx/getAddressEx.ino
  4. examples/Struct_writer/Struct_writer.ino

Use Example Sketches Guide for a full breakdown of what each sketch tests, what success looks like, and how to use failures for troubleshooting.

docs.title: 5. If it fails
docs.text:
• Call flash.error(VERBOSE).
  • Check Errors and Diagnostics.
  • Run diagnostics example and capture output.
  • Follow the troubleshooting workflow in Example Sketches Guide.

docs.title: Critical code paths
docs.text:
• SPIFlash::_prep and _addressCheck
  • SPIFlash::_chipID and SFDP branch
  • architecture-specific SPI transaction start/end
  • FRAM identification and capacity derivation

docs.title: Adding chip support
docs.text:
• update ID/capacity detection
  • define erase/program behavior and timing
  • validate read/write/erase/power flows on hardware
  • update docs and examples in same change

docs.title: Regression focus
docs.text:
• page-boundary writes
  • overflow behavior
  • suspend/resume semantics
  • power-down/power-up semantics

docs.title: Docs
docs.text:
• compatibility matrix updated
  • API reference updated for all changed methods
  • docs build passes in CI

docs.title: Validation
docs.text:
• compile examples across representative boards
  • verify read/write/erase on at least one flash and one FRAM part
  • verify diagnostics still map correctly

docs.title: Publish
docs.text:
• merge to main branch
  • confirm docs publish workflow succeeded
  • verify site URL and key links

docs.title: Chip does not respond
docs.text:
• verify wiring and voltage levels
  • verify CS pin selection
  • lower SPI clock and retest
  • ensure begin() is called before IO

docs.title: Write failures
docs.text:
• erase flash region before writing
  • disable HIGHSPEED while debugging
  • check address boundaries and overflow configuration

docs.title: Intermittent behavior
docs.text:
• verify shared SPI bus ownership
  • verify board-core transaction compatibility
  • enable diagnostics and capture error code sequence
